content.text: Palampur is a hypothetical village that introduces basic concepts relating to production. It is well-connected with neighbouring villages and towns. Raiganj, a big village, is 3 km away, connected by an all-weather road. Further connectivity leads to the small town of Shahpur.

Various modes of transport are visible on this road—bullock carts, tongas, bogeys (wooden carts drawn by buffalos) loaded with jaggery, as well as motor vehicles like motorcycles, jeeps, tractors, and trucks.

The village has about 450 families belonging to several castes. Eighty upper caste families own the majority of land and live in brick houses with cement plastering. Scheduled Castes (dalits) comprise one-third of the population, living in one corner in smaller houses, some made of mud and straw.

content.title: Facilities in Palampur
items: Electricity: Powers all tubewells and small businesses; most houses have electric connections
Education: Two primary schools and one high school
Health: One primary health centre (government) and one private dispensary

content.text: The aim of production is to produce goods and services that we want. There are four requirements for production of goods and services, known as factors of production.

content.title: Four Factors of Production
items: Land: Includes land and other natural resources such as water, forests, minerals
Labour: People who will do the work—some activities require highly educated workers, others require manual workers
Physical Capital: The variety of inputs required at every stage during production
Human Capital: Knowledge and enterprise to combine land, labour, and physical capital to produce output

topics.topic_name: Physical Capital: Fixed and Working Capital

content.text: Physical capital is divided into two categories based on how they are used in production.

content.title: Fixed Capital
content.text: Tools, machines, and buildings that can be used in production over many years. Examples include a farmer's plough, generators, turbines, computers, tractors, and threshers. These are not used up in a single production cycle.

content.title: Working Capital
content.text: Raw materials and money in hand that are used up in production. Examples include yarn used by a weaver, clay used by a potter, seeds, fertilizers, pesticides, and cash for making payments. These are consumed during the production process.

Farming is the main production activity in Palampur, with 75% of working people dependent on farming for their livelihood. Since 1960, there has been no expansion in land area under cultivation. Wastelands had already been converted to cultivable land, leaving no further scope to increase farm production by bringing new land under cultivation.

content.title: Units of Land Measurement
content.text: The standard unit of measuring land is hectare. One hectare equals the area of a square with one side measuring 100 metres. In villages, local units such as bigha and guintha are also used.

Farmers in Palampur grow three different crops in a year due to the well-developed system of irrigation. Electricity transformed irrigation—Persian wheels were replaced by electric-run tubewells that could irrigate much larger areas. By mid-1970s, the entire cultivated area of 200 hectares was irrigated.

content.title: Cropping Pattern in Palampur
items: Kharif (Rainy Season): Jowar and bajra (used as cattle feed)
Rabi (Winter Season): Wheat—farmers keep enough for family consumption and sell the surplus at Raiganj market
Sugarcane: Grown on part of land, harvested once a year; sold as raw form or jaggery to traders in Shahpur

content.title: Multiple Cropping
content.text: Growing more than one crop on a piece of land during the year is known as multiple cropping. It is the most common way of increasing production on a given piece of land. All farmers in Palampur grow at least two main crops; many grow potato as the third crop.

topics.topic_name: Modern Farming Methods and Green Revolution

content.text: The other way to increase production is to use modern farming methods for higher yield. Yield is measured as crop produced on a given piece of land during a single season.

Till the mid-1960s, farmers used traditional seeds with relatively low yields. These required less irrigation and farmers used cow-dung and natural manure as fertilizers, which were readily available without cost.

content.title: Green Revolution
content.text: The Green Revolution in the late 1960s introduced High Yielding Varieties (HYV) of seeds for wheat and rice. HYV seeds promised much greater amounts of grain on a single plant. However, they required plenty of water, chemical fertilizers, and pesticides to produce best results.

content.title: Impact on Wheat Yield in Palampur
content.text: Traditional varieties yielded 1300 kg per hectare. With HYV seeds, the yield increased to 3200 kg per hectare—a significant increase in wheat production.

Farmers of Punjab, Haryana, and Western Uttar Pradesh were the first to adopt modern farming methods. They set up tubewells, used HYV seeds, chemical fertilizers, pesticides, and bought farm machinery like tractors and threshers, leading to high yields of wheat.

Modern farming methods have overused the natural resource base. Green Revolution is associated with loss of soil fertility due to increased use of chemical fertilizers. Continuous use of groundwater for tubewell irrigation has led to depletion of the water-table.

Chemical fertilizers provide minerals that dissolve in water and are immediately available to plants, but they may escape from soil and pollute groundwater, rivers, and lakes. They can also kill bacteria and other micro-organisms in the soil, making the soil less fertile over time.

content.title: Punjab Case
content.text: Punjab has the highest consumption of chemical fertilizers. Continuous use has led to degradation of soil health. Farmers are forced to use more and more chemical fertilizers and inputs to achieve the same production level, causing cost of cultivation to rise very fast.

Land distribution in Palampur is highly unequal. Out of 450 families: 150 families (one-third, mostly dalits) are landless; 240 families cultivate small plots of land less than 2 hectares; 60 families are medium and large farmers cultivating more than 2 hectares, with a few having land over 10 hectares.

content.title: Gobind's Family
content.text: Gobind had 2.25 hectares of largely unirrigated land. After his death, the land was divided among his three sons, each getting only 0.75 hectare. Even with improved irrigation and modern methods, they cannot make a living from their land and must look for additional work.
Small farmers cultivate their own fields along with their families, providing the labour required themselves. Medium and large farmers hire farm labourers to work on their fields.

Farm labourers come from landless families or families cultivating small plots. Unlike farmers, they do not have a right over the crops grown. They are paid wages in cash or in kind (e.g., crop), and sometimes receive meals. Wages vary by region, crop, and farm activity.

content.title: Dala and Ramkali
content.text: Dala is a landless farm labourer who works on daily wages. Government minimum wage is Rs 300 per day (March 2019), but Dala gets only Rs 160 due to heavy competition for work. Both Dala and Ramkali are among the poorest people in the village.
Modern farming methods require a great deal of capital. Small farmers often need to borrow money to arrange for capital.

content.title: Savita's Story
content.text: Savita, a small farmer with 1 hectare, needs working capital of Rs 3,000 for seeds, fertilizers, pesticides, water, and repairs. She borrows from Tejpal Singh, a large farmer, at 24% interest for four months. She also must work on his field during harvest at Rs 100 per day (low wage). She agrees to these tough conditions because getting a loan is difficult for small farmers.
In contrast, medium and large farmers have their own savings from farming and can arrange capital themselves. Their surplus production allows them to save and invest.

Farmers retain part of the wheat for family consumption and sell the surplus. Small farmers have little surplus because their total production is small and a substantial share is kept for family needs. Medium and large farmers supply wheat to the market.

content.title: Tejpal Singh
content.text: Tejpal Singh, a large farmer, has a surplus of 350 quintals of wheat. He sells it at Raiganj market, earning good returns. He deposits most money in his bank account, uses savings to lend to small farmers like Savita, arranges working capital for next season, and plans to buy another tractor (fixed capital).
Only 25% of working people in Palampur are engaged in activities other than agriculture. These non-farm activities include dairy, small-scale manufacturing, shopkeeping, and transport.

Dairy is a common activity in many families. People feed buffalos on grass and jowar/bajra grown during rainy season. Milk is sold in Raiganj. Two traders from Shahpur have set up collection-cum-chilling centres at Raiganj, from where milk is transported to faraway towns and cities.

Less than fifty people are engaged in manufacturing. Unlike big factories, manufacturing in Palampur involves simple production methods done on a small scale, mostly at home or in fields with family labour. Rarely are labourers hired.

content.title: Mishrilal's Sugarcane Crushing Unit
content.text: Mishrilal purchased a mechanical sugarcane crushing machine run on electricity. He buys sugarcane from other farmers and processes it into jaggery, which he sells to traders at Shahpur, making a small profit.

Shopkeepers buy various goods from wholesale markets in cities and sell them in the village. Small general stores sell a wide range of items—rice, wheat, sugar, tea, oil, biscuits, soap, toothpaste, batteries, candles, notebooks, pen, pencil, cloth. Families whose houses are close to the bus stand have opened small shops selling eatables.

content.title: Kareem's Computer Class Centre
content.text: Kareem opened a computer class centre in the village. Many students attend college in Shahpur town and were also attending computer classes there. Kareem employed two women in the village with computer degrees, bought computers, and set up classes. High school students now attend in good numbers.

Various vehicles operate on the road connecting Palampur to Raiganj—rickshawallahs, tongawallahs, jeep drivers, tractor drivers, truck drivers, and people driving bullock carts and bogeys. They ferry people and goods for payment. The number of people involved in transport has grown over the years.

content.title: Kishora's Story
content.text: Kishora, a landless farm labourer, found it difficult to meet family needs from wages. He took a loan from a bank under a government programme providing cheap loans to poor landless households. He bought a buffalo with this money, sells its milk, and attached a wooden cart to it for transport. He now transports clay for the potter, jaggery, and other commodities. He earns more than before.

glossary.definition: The four requirements for production of goods and services: land, labour, physical capital, and human capital.
Tools, machines, and buildings that can be used in production over many years. Examples include plough, tractors, generators, and computers.
Raw materials and money in hand that are used up in production. Examples include seeds, fertilizers, pesticides, and cash.
Growing more than one crop on a piece of land during the year. It is the most common way of increasing production on a given piece of land.
The crop produced on a given piece of land during a single season.
Seeds introduced during the Green Revolution that produce much greater amounts of grain on a single plant but require plenty of water, chemical fertilizers, and pesticides.

The period in the late 1960s when Indian farmers were introduced to cultivation of wheat and rice using High Yielding Varieties (HYV) of seeds, leading to significantly increased food grain production.
The rainy season cropping season in India. Crops like jowar and bajra are grown during this season.
The winter season cropping season in India. Wheat is the main crop grown during this season.
The portion of farm produce that remains after keeping enough for the family's consumption, which is sold in the market.
Households that do not own any land for cultivation. In Palampur, 150 out of 450 families are landless.
Production activities other than farming, including dairy, small-scale manufacturing, shopkeeping, and transport.
The variety of inputs required at every stage during production, including fixed capital (tools, machines, buildings) and working capital (raw materials, money).
The knowledge and enterprise needed to combine land, labour, and physical capital to produce output.

summary_points: Palampur is a hypothetical village with 450 families; 80 upper caste families own most land while one-third of the population (dalits) are landless; the village has good infrastructure—all-weather roads, electricity, schools, and health centres.

The four factors of production are: land (natural resources), labour (workers), physical capital (fixed capital like tools and machines; working capital like raw materials and money), and human capital (knowledge and enterprise).

Farming is the main activity (75% of workers). Land area is fixed—no expansion since 1960. Multiple cropping (growing 2-3 crops per year) and modern farming methods (HYV seeds, chemical fertilizers, irrigation) have increased production.
Multiple cropping uses kharif crops (jowar, bajra), potato (October-December), and rabi crops (wheat). Modern methods increased wheat yield from 1300 kg/hectare to 3200 kg/hectare.
The Green Revolution (late 1960s) introduced HYV seeds requiring water, chemical fertilizers, and pesticides. While increasing production, it caused soil fertility loss and groundwater depletion.

Land distribution is unequal: 150 families landless, 240 families have small plots (<2 hectares), 60 families are medium/large farmers (>2 hectares). Small farmers struggle to make a living; many face debt and low income.
Labour: Small farmers use family labour; large farmers hire landless labourers like Dala who earn below minimum wage (Rs 160 vs government Rs 300). Wages are low due to competition for limited work.
Capital: Small farmers like Savita borrow at high interest (24%) and face harsh conditions. Large farmers like Tejpal Singh have savings from surplus production and can finance farming themselves.

Non-farm activities engage 25% of workers: dairy (milk sold in Raiganj), small-scale manufacturing (Mishrilal's jaggery production), shopkeeping (general stores, Kareem's computer classes), and transport (Kishora's buffalo cart business).

Non-farm activities require little land but need capital—often obtained through loans. Better roads and connectivity to towns and cities (Raiganj, Shahpur) provide markets for non-farm products and services.
